subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Diagrams are relevant, accurate and correctly labelled with all key elements (MPB/MPC/MSB, social optimum, DWL) present.
Graphical representation clearly illustrates the externality wedge and welfare loss.
Minor spelling errors do not obscure the correctness of the diagram.
weaknesses: Explanation of the diagram remains somewhat brief and could benefit from deeper interpretation of each shift.
Inconsistency between consumption vs production externality context.
Missing a legend or colour differentiation to distinguish curves visually.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the studentâs explanation is not fully developed, the two externality diagrams are highly accurate, correctly labelled, and include all essential elements. We award the top mark for Diagrams to reflect the high quality of diagram inclusion and labelling, acknowledging only minor points for improvement.

strengths: Consistent and appropriate use of technical terms such as ânegative externalityâ, âallocative inefficiencyâ, and âdeadweight lossâ.
Terminology is accurate and used correctly in context throughout the commentary.
weaknesses: Occasional spelling errors (e.g. ârequlatorsâ) detract slightly from professionalism.
A more formal tone in places (e.g. avoid casual phrasing like âdrawbacksâ) would enhance clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates thorough and accurate use of economic terminology throughout the work, meeting the highest band for Terminology. Minor spelling and tone issues do not detract from the overall precision of terms used.

strengths: Relevant economic theory of negative externalities, subsidies, and market failure is applied consistently to the article.
Real-world policy example (Thames Tideway Scheme) effectively illustrates corrective interventions.
Ecological details (e.g. biological oxygen demand) are linked back to economic analysis.
weaknesses: Analytical depth is uneven, with some assertions (e.g. impact on fishing) lacking quantitative support.
Scope is largely confined to one framework, without exploring alternative theoretical lenses (e.g. property rights).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While some analysis remains superficial, the continuous application of economic theory, supported by a well-chosen real-world example and ecological insights, justifies the top band. Awarding 3 marks balances the studentâs strong application with areas for deepening analytical rigour.

strengths: Key concept of sustainability is clearly identified and frames the discussion.
Repeated references tie sustainability to environmental and intergenerational issues raised in the article.
weaknesses: Connections between evaluation points and sustainability could be more explicitly and systematically developed.
Some discussion sections revert to economic analysis without explicitly linking back to the key concept.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student identifies sustainability appropriately and makes consistent attempts to link it to the article. However, the connection is not fully elaborated at every turn, placing this work solidly in the middle band for Key Concept.

strengths: Judgments on policy options (subsidy vs regulation) are clearly stated.
Use of shareholder dividend data provides an incisive critique of firmsâ incentives.
weaknesses: Reasoning supporting judgments is limited and would benefit from deeper cost-benefit trade-off analysis.
Evaluations are one-sided; alternative perspectives (e.g. equity concerns) are not fully considered.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The commentary includes valid judgments and provides supporting reasoning, though both are relatively thin and lack balance. Awarding 2 marks reflects effective but under-developed evaluation, in line with the evidence provided.

strandSelections.reasoning: The commentary presents some analytical points â linking sewage discharges to negative externalities, identifying DWL, describing subsidy effects â but development is shallow.  Numerical or step-by-step reasoning is absent, and claims (e.g. impact on fishing sector or household welfare) are asserted rather than analysed.  Therefore the analysis is âlimited or superficialâ, matching the 1-mark band.

The student provides two clear negative-externality diagrams with Price and Quantity axes, MPB/MPC/MSB curves, social optimum, PE/QE, DWL and the externality wedge all accurately drawn and labelled.  Minor weaknesses (e.g. âExtrenalityâ spelling error, MSB drawn for a consumption externality while the text mixes production/consumption problems) do not distort the overall accuracy, so the work meets the descriptor for ârelevant, accurate and correctly labelledâ but does not exceed it.

Links between sustainability and the article are made repeatedly (threats to human health, ecosystems, future water availability) and tied to the externality analysis, demonstrating a partial but not yet âfull and clearâ explanation.  For example, the commentary does not explicitly integrate each evaluation point back to the concept, so the work fits the middle band.

Relevant theory (negative externalities of consumption, subsidy and regulation as corrective policies, allocative inefficiency, DWL) is applied to the article from start to finish.  However, the scope is limited to a single market-failure framework and does not extend to alternative lenses (e.g. property rights, pricing power, cost-benefit).  Hence it meets âapplied throughoutâ but not the âthoroughlyâ criterion required for the top band.

Reasoning behind the judgments is stated (âshareholder dividends show profit could be reinvestedâ, âsubsidy shifts MSBâ) but remains thin, qualitative and sometimes speculative.  There is little depth, comparison or reference to data; consequently, supporting reasoning is âlimitedâ, fitting the 1-mark band.

The student clearly and appropriately identifies âSustainabilityâ as the key concept and explicitly frames the whole discussion around the long-run ability to meet current and future needs.  This is a suitable and distinct concept for the first IA, fully satisfying the top-band identification criterion.

The student explains the key features of the diagram â equilibrium, external cost, over-use, higher social price and DWL â but the discussion remains brief and contains conceptual confusion (the student admits the graph âcannot capture the essenceâ, and mixes statements about over-allocation with ânot too much sewageâ).  This constitutes a limited, partially accurate explanation rather than the âfull and accurateâ detail required for the top band.

Throughout the commentary the student consistently uses terms such as negative externality, marginal private/social benefit, marginal private cost, deadweight loss, allocative inefficiency, subsidy and market failure correctly and in context.  Mis-spelled words (âreqlulatorsâ) and an occasional awkward phrasing do not undermine generally appropriate usage, so the work satisfies the highest terminology band.

Judgments are present (e.g. subsidy may reduce externality but problems remain; enforcement of existing laws preferred) but are brief and one-sided, lacking explicit balance or weighing of alternatives.  This matches the descriptor of âlimited judgmentsâ.

annotations.comment: Enhance evaluation by weighing the trade-offs of allowing price increases, such as potential equity concerns for low-income households.
The student clearly identifies sustainability as the central key concept, framing subsequent discussion appropriately.
To strengthen the connection, explicitly link sustainability to specific article examples throughout the analysis rather than stating the concept in isolation.
Effective use of article data in the opening to provide context and quantify the scale of waterway pollution.
The header contains a spelling error ârequlatorsâ; ensuring correct terminology enhances professionalism and clarity.
Consider developing deeper analysis of how dividend payouts could impact long-term reinvestment incentives and environmental outcomes.
Introduce relevant economic theories (e.g., cost-benefit analysis of infrastructure upgrades) when outlining potential solutions.
Strong real-world policy example (Thames Tideway Scheme) effectively illustrates a corrective intervention and its promise.
Terminology such as allocative inefficiency and negative externality is used accurately to convey key microeconomic concepts.
The negative externality diagram is correctly labelled but would benefit from a legend or colour coding to distinguish MPB, MSB, and MPC curves clearly.
The description of MPC includes an awkward phrase âproviding maintaining and drainage systemsâ; consider clarifying the supply curveâs interpretation.
Align the externality type (consumption vs production) consistently by adjusting the graph or text to reflect the sewage disposal context.
Rather than dismissing the graphâs utility, explicitly discuss its limitations and how it approximates the real-world externality.
When citing policy directives, explain their specific benchmarks and implications for market outcomes.
Assess the Thames Tideway Schemeâs economic efficiency by weighing its construction costs against projected social benefits.
Including a subsidy diagram to show shifts in MSB or MPC would strengthen the analysis of subsidiesâ effects.
Claims about impacts on agriculture and fishing sectors are asserted without quantitative evidence or detailed causation; deepen analysis with data.
Insightful use of shareholder dividend figures to critique firmsâ incentive structures in addressing the externality.
Quantify the proportion of household income spent on water bills to substantiate claims about consumer welfare impacts.
Nicely brings the discussion back to sustainability, framing environmental outcomes in an intergenerational context.
Introduce counterarguments or compare alternative policies to create more balanced and nuanced evaluations.
Effective use of subheadings (âBad news for swimmers and natureâ) to guide reader through the articleâs thematic structure.
Good application of ecological concepts like biological oxygen demand to explain environmental impacts.
Link these ecological effects back to allocative inefficiency and welfare analysis to deepen the economic interpretation.
Consider refining the language in the introduction for formal tone and clarity, avoiding casual phrasing like âdrawbacksâ.
